feedback.text: Dear European Commission, I am writing to respectfully urge you to include provisions to prevent video game publishers from retroactively shutting down video games that they no longer want to support. In recent years, hundreds of online games have been rendered entirely unplayable due to the publishers shutting down their servers. This includes high-profile titles like The Crew (published by Ubisoft), Call of Duty: Warzone (Activision) and more recently Resident Evil: ReVerse (Capcom). Many other games, including Anthem (EA) are at risk of being shut down within the next few years. Consumers often spend a significant amount of money on these games, purchasing cosmetic items, downloadable content, or season passes which can cost them hundreds of euros. This is besides the initial purchase price, which can range from 60 to 80. In all the cases that I have mentioned, there was no recourse provided to the consumers for their lost access or items once the games were shut down. This is a clear violation of basic consumer rights. The solution need not require publishers to continue to support these games indefinitely with their own resources and money. It only requires that publishers implement end-of-life measures at minimal or no cost to them, such as setting up an offline mode or allowing fans to maintain their own servers. Stopping the practice of retroactively shutting down video games is thus in line with the core aims of the Digital Fairness Act and should be included within it. I am respectfully urging the European Commission to take this opportunity to safeguard digital ownership and basic consumer rights.
